name: Claypot Rice
description: Cantonese claypot rice with cured sausage, pork belly, and a crispy golden crust.
recipe: Rinse 350g jasmine rice and soak in cold water for 40 minutes, then drain well. Soaking ensures even cooking regardless of rice variety.
Blanch Chinese kale in salted boiling water with a small splash of baijiu (white spirits) for 10 seconds until bright green. Immediately transfer to cold water to stop cooking and keep it crisp.

Blanch 150g Chinese sausage and cured pork belly together in boiling water for 2 minutes to clean and draw out their aroma. While still hot, peel the casing off the sausage, then slice both on a diagonal.

Make the claypot sauce: fry sliced scallion, 2 garlic cloves, a small piece of onion, 1 star anise, 1 bay leaf, and a pinch of fennel seeds in oil over medium heat until golden. Add 3 tbsp light soy sauce, ½ tbsp oyster sauce, ½ tbsp dark soy sauce, and 1 tsp sugar. Stir, add 4 tbsp water, bring to a boil, and simmer 15 seconds. Strain and set aside.
Generously grease a clay pot — bottom and sides — with lard or peanut oil (about 4 spoonfuls). This prevents sticking and builds the signature crust.
Add 400g cold water to the greased pot and bring to a boil over high heat. Just as the edges begin to bubble (before a full rolling boil), pour in the drained rice and spread it flat with a spatula.
Cook uncovered on high heat until the water reaches a full boil. Reduce to medium-low, cover with the lid slightly ajar, and steam for 2 minutes until almost all the water is absorbed.
Quickly arrange the sliced sausage and pork on top of the rice. Drizzle oil in a circle along the inner edge of the pot. Cover fully and reduce to the lowest heat setting.
Rotate the pot 90° every 3 minutes so each quarter of the bottom faces the flame — 4 rotations × 3 minutes = 12 minutes total. This evenly crisps all sides of the crust without burning any spot.

Turn off the heat. Drizzle the claypot sauce generously over the rice, top with the blanched greens, and toss everything together. Scrape up the golden crunchy crust from the bottom — that's the best part.
